title: Generic logger injection should match enclosing type
description: In most logging frameworks, it’s good practice to set the logger name to match its enclosing type, as enforced by S3416. Logging frameworks can define or use Generic interfaces for the logger, such as ILogger<TCategoryName>. The use of a logger of a generic type parameter A (e.g.
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

logic.detection_conditions: Detects ILogger<T> fields where T doesn't match the enclosing class name
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when: ILogger<ClassName> is declared in a class with a different name
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes: ILogger<T> where T matches enclosing class, non-generic ILogger, comments
never_trigger_on: ILogger<T> where T matches the enclosing class
logic.reasoning: This rule requires semantic analysis to properly verify that the logger generic type matches the enclosing class. A simplified regex-based approach can only detect the presence of ILogger<T> declarations but cannot reliably validate the type parameter matches the class name due to: 1) Class name must be extracted from declaration, 2) ILogger type parameter must be extracted, 3) They must be compared. This requires parsing context that regex cannot provide. A full implementation needs semantic analysis or a custom function. This placeholder pattern detects ILogger<T> declarations for basic identification.
